title: Web Development Fundamentals
description: A comprehensive foundation covering how the web works, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, TypeScript, React, tooling, and design patterns -- everything you need to build modern web applications from scratch.
keyPoints: The web works through DNS resolution, TCP connections, and the HTTP request/response cycle
Browsers parse HTML into the DOM, CSS into the CSSOM, then layout, paint, and composite to render pages
Semantic HTML (header, nav, main, article, section, aside, footer) improves accessibility and SEO
ARIA attributes supplement native HTML semantics for screen readers and assistive technology
The CSS box model (content, padding, border, margin) determines element sizing -- always use border-box
Flexbox handles one-dimensional layouts (rows or columns); CSS Grid handles two-dimensional layouts
Responsive design uses media queries, relative units, and flexible layouts to adapt to any screen size
CSS variables enable theming and maintainable design tokens across your entire stylesheet
JavaScript const/let replace var; destructuring, spread, and optional chaining simplify code
Closures let inner functions remember variables from their outer scope -- used extensively in React hooks
async/await with try/catch is the modern pattern for handling asynchronous operations
Array methods (map, filter, reduce, find) are the foundation of data transformation in React
TypeScript catches bugs at edit time with type annotations, interfaces, generics, and utility types
Discriminated unions and type narrowing make impossible states unrepresentable in TypeScript
React components are functions that return JSX; they receive data through props
useState provides local state; useEffect handles side effects; useRef stores mutable values
useMemo and useCallback optimize performance by caching values and function references
Context API broadcasts shared state (theme, auth, locale) without prop drilling
Custom hooks extract reusable stateful logic and are the modern replacement for HOCs and render props
npm, yarn, and pnpm manage dependencies; semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) communicates changes
VS Code with ESLint, Prettier, and TypeScript gives you real-time error checking and auto-formatting
Browser DevTools (Elements, Console, Network, Performance) are essential for debugging
Git tracks changes, enables branching, and is the foundation of team collaboration
The Fetch API makes HTTP requests; LocalStorage persists data; WebSockets enable real-time communication
Component composition (small, composable parts) is preferred over monolithic components with many props

blocks.title: You don't need to memorize everything
blocks.body: Nobody memorizes every HTML tag, CSS property, or JavaScript method. Professional developers look things up constantly. The goal of this page is to build a mental map of what exists so you know what to search for. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a refresher.

cards.title: HTML
cards.body: The structure and content of every web page. HTML elements define headings, paragraphs, links, images, forms, and the overall document outline.

cards.title: CSS
cards.body: The visual presentation layer. CSS controls colors, fonts, spacing, layout (Flexbox and Grid), animations, and responsive design across screen sizes.

cards.title: JavaScript
cards.body: The programming language of the web. JavaScript handles interactivity, data fetching, DOM manipulation, and powers frameworks like React.

cards.title: TypeScript
cards.body: A typed superset of JavaScript that catches bugs before your code runs. Used by most modern teams and required by many job postings.

cards.title: React
cards.body: The most popular UI library. React lets you build interfaces from small, reusable components with a declarative approach to managing state and rendering.

cards.title: Tooling
cards.body: VS Code, Git, npm, ESLint, Prettier, and browser DevTools form the everyday toolkit that makes you productive and your code reliable.

blocks.title: Quick Reference: Semantic HTML Page Structure
blocks.code:
Home Blog About

Page Title

Main content goes here.

Subsection

Group related content with section elements.

Sidebar

Related links, ads, or supplementary info.

© 2025 My Website

blocks.heading: HTML Tags Cheat Sheet
rows: Hyperlink to another page or resource

blocks.title: Quick Reference: CSS Flexbox Centering & Spacing
Quick Reference: CSS Grid 2-Column Layout

blocks.body:
**Use Flexbox when** you are arranging items in a single direction -- either a row or a column. Flexbox is ideal for:

- Navigation bars
- Centering content vertically and horizontally
- Distributing space between items
- Aligning items of different sizes
- Card rows that wrap on small screens

Think of Flexbox as a **clothesline**: items are hung along one line (horizontal or vertical) and you control spacing and alignment along that line.

**Use CSS Grid when** you need to control both rows AND columns at the same time. Grid is ideal for:

- Full page layouts (header, sidebar, content, footer)
- Card grids with consistent sizing
- Dashboard-style interfaces
- Any layout where items need to align on both axes

Think of Grid as a **spreadsheet**: you define rows and columns, then place items into cells.

blocks.heading: CSS Properties Beginners Use Most
rows: Sets the layout mode of an element

blocks.title: Quick Reference: Variables, Functions & Arrow Functions
Quick Reference: async/await Fetch Example
Quick Reference: Array Methods (map, filter, find)
Common Beginner Mistakes to Avoid
blocks.body:
**1. Using == instead of ===** -- Double equals does type coercion (`'5' == 5` is true). Always use strict equality (`===`).

**2. Forgetting that map/filter return new arrays** -- They do not modify the original. You must use the return value: `const result = arr.filter(...)`, not just `arr.filter(...)`.

**3. Mutating state directly in React** -- Never do `state.push(item)`. Use the setter: `setState([...state, item])`.

**4. Missing the dependency array in useEffect** -- An empty `[]` runs once on mount. Omitting it entirely runs on every render and can cause infinite loops.

**5. Not handling async errors** -- Always wrap `await` calls in try/catch or use `.catch()`. Unhandled promise rejections crash Node and confuse users.

blocks.heading: JavaScript vs TypeScript Comparison
rows: Static -- types checked at compile time
function add(a: number, b: number): number { ... }
interface User { name: string; age: number; }
function identity<T>(value: T): T { return value; }
Strict null checks catch it at compile time
Small overhead that pays off quickly
Compiles to JavaScript -- runs everywhere too
Any project you plan to maintain or grow

blocks.title: Quick Reference: React useState + useEffect
You are making great progress
blocks.body: If you have read through HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and React above, you already know the core building blocks of modern web development. The sections below cover the tools and workflows that tie everything together. These are less conceptual and more practical -- install the tools, try the commands, and build something.

blocks.title: What to build first
blocks.body: The best way to solidify everything on this page is to build a small project. Start with something you will actually use: a personal homepage, a to-do app, or a bookmark manager. Keep it simple -- one page, a few components, maybe a fetch call to a public API. You will look things up constantly, and that is completely normal.

references.title: HTML and CSS: Design and Build Websites
